words.exampleEN: Health is the most important thing.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. The 'ei' sounds like 'eye'. Also said when someone sneezes, like 'Bless you!'

words.exampleNL: Ik ben ziek, ik kan niet werken.
words.exampleEN: I'm sick, I can't work.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee' in 'seek'. Simple pronunciation.

words.exampleEN: Eating vegetables is healthy.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. The 'd' at the end sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb pijn in mijn rug.
words.exampleEN: I have pain in my back.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'. Key word for doctor visits.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb een afspraak bij de huisarts.
words.exampleEN: I have an appointment with the GP.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'huis' (house) + 'arts' (doctor). The 'ui' sounds like 'ow' with rounded lips. The GP is the first point of contact in Dutch healthcare.

words.exampleNL: De dokter zegt dat ik moet rusten.
words.exampleEN: The doctor says I need to rest.
words.audioHint: Similar to English 'doctor'. The 'o' is short.

words.exampleNL: Zij is arts in het ziekenhuis.
words.exampleEN: She is a physician at the hospital.
words.audioHint: More formal than 'dokter'. The 'a' sounds like 'ah'.

words.exampleNL: Hij is opgenomen in het ziekenhuis.
words.exampleEN: He has been admitted to the hospital.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'zieken' (sick people) + 'huis' (house). The 'ui' sounds like 'ow' with rounded lips.

words.exampleNL: Ik haal mijn medicijnen bij de apotheek.
words.exampleEN: I pick up my medicine at the pharmacy.
words.audioHint: Stress on the last syllable. The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'.

words.exampleNL: De dokter schrijft een recept voor.
words.exampleEN: The doctor writes a prescription.
words.audioHint: Same word for both prescription and recipe. Context determines meaning.

words.exampleNL: Neem dit medicijn drie keer per dag.
words.exampleEN: Take this medicine three times a day.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' at the end sounds like 'eye'. Stress on the last syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb pijn in mijn hoofd.
words.exampleEN: I have a headache.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. The 'fd' at the end sounds like 'ft'.

words.exampleEN: I have a terrible headache.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'hoofd' (head) + 'pijn' (pain). The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'.

words.exampleEN: My stomach hurts.
words.audioHint: The 'ui' is a unique Dutch diphthong - say 'ow' but with rounded lips.

words.exampleEN: The child has a stomachache.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'buik' (belly) + 'pijn' (pain).

words.exampleNL: Ik heb last van mijn rug.
words.exampleEN: I have trouble with my back.
words.audioHint: The 'u' sounds like 'u' in 'put'. The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: I broke my leg.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' sounds like 'ay' in 'day'. Can also mean 'bone' in some contexts.

words.exampleNL: Mijn arm doet pijn na de vaccinatie.
words.exampleEN: My arm hurts after the vaccination.
words.audioHint: Pronounced like English 'arm' but with a slightly rolled 'r'.

words.exampleEN: Wash your hands well.
words.audioHint: The 'a' sounds like 'ah'. The 'd' sounds like 't' at the end.

words.exampleNL: Mijn voeten doen pijn van het lopen.
words.exampleEN: My feet hurt from walking.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'food'. The 't' is clearly pronounced.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb last van mijn knie.
words.exampleEN: I have trouble with my knee.
words.audioHint: The 'k' is pronounced before the 'n', unlike in English 'knee'. The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb pijn in mijn schouder.
words.exampleEN: I have pain in my shoulder.
words.audioHint: The 'sch' sounds like 'skh'. The 'ou' sounds like 'ow' in 'cow'.

words.exampleEN: I have a stiff neck.
words.audioHint: The 'e' sounds like 'e' in 'neck'. Very similar to English.

words.exampleEN: I have an earache.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. Simple pronunciation.

words.exampleNL: Mijn oog is rood en doet pijn.
words.exampleEN: My eye is red and hurts.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. The final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: My nose is congested.
words.audioHint: The 'eu' is a unique Dutch sound - round lips for 'oo' but say 'uh'.

words.exampleNL: Doe je mond open, zegt de tandarts.
words.exampleEN: Open your mouth, says the dentist.
words.audioHint: The 'o' is short. The 'd' at the end sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb kiespijn, mijn tand doet pijn.
words.exampleEN: I have a toothache, my tooth hurts.
words.audioHint: The 'a' sounds like 'ah'. The final 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Ik ga twee keer per jaar naar de tandarts.
words.exampleEN: I go to the dentist twice a year.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'tand' (tooth) + 'arts' (doctor).

words.exampleEN: I have a sore throat.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' sounds like 'ay' in 'day'. 'Keelpijn' means sore throat.

words.exampleEN: The child has a high fever.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. No plural as it's uncountable.

words.exampleEN: I have a cold.
words.audioHint: The 'ou' sounds like 'ow'. The 'ei' in '-heid' sounds like 'eye'.

words.exampleEN: I've been coughing for three days.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'boot'.

words.exampleNL: Ik moet steeds niezen door mijn allergie.
words.exampleEN: I keep sneezing because of my allergy.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'. The 'z' is soft.

words.exampleNL: Veel mensen hebben de griep dit jaar.
words.exampleEN: Many people have the flu this year.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb een allergie voor noten.
words.exampleEN: I have a nut allergy.
words.audioHint: The 'g' is guttural. Stress on the last syllable.

words.exampleNL: Ik wil een afspraak maken bij de huisarts.
words.exampleEN: I want to make an appointment with the GP.
words.audioHint: The 'aa' is a long 'ah'. 'Een afspraak maken' means to make an appointment.

words.exampleEN: Do you have health insurance?
words.audioHint: The 'v' sounds like 'f'. Health insurance is mandatory in the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: Een zorgverzekering is verplicht in Nederland.
words.exampleEN: Health insurance is mandatory in the Netherlands.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'zorg' (care) + 'verzekering' (insurance). Very important for life in NL.

words.exampleEN: The nurse takes blood.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. The 'd' at the end sounds like 't'.

words.exampleEN: The nurse measures my blood pressure.
words.audioHint: The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'. This is the official gender-neutral term.

words.exampleEN: Take two pills per day.
words.audioHint: The 'i' sounds like 'i' in 'pill'. Same as English.

words.exampleEN: Take a tablet with water.
words.audioHint: Similar to English. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Wilt u in de wachtkamer plaatsnemen?
words.exampleEN: Would you take a seat in the waiting room?
words.audioHint: Compound of 'wacht' (wait) + 'kamer' (room). The 'w' sounds like 'v'.

words.exampleNL: Ga naar de spoedeisende hulp als het erger wordt.
words.exampleEN: Go to the ER if it gets worse.
words.audioHint: Often abbreviated as 'SEH'. The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. The 'ei' sounds like 'eye'.

words.exampleEN: Call 112 for an ambulance.
words.audioHint: Similar to English but with French-style pronunciation. 112 is the emergency number in the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: Ik heb pijn op mijn borst.
words.exampleEN: I have chest pain.
words.audioHint: The 'o' is short. Simple pronunciation.

words.exampleEN: My heart is beating very fast.
words.audioHint: The 'a' sounds like 'ah'. Similar to English 'heart'.

words.exampleNL: Roken is slecht voor de longen.
words.exampleEN: Smoking is bad for the lungs.
words.audioHint: The 'o' is short. The 'ng' sounds like 'ng' in 'song'.

words.exampleEN: I have acid reflux.
words.audioHint: The 'aa' is a long 'ah'. The final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: I cut my finger.
words.audioHint: The 'v' is soft. The 'i' sounds like 'i' in 'sing'.

words.exampleNL: Mijn huid is droog in de winter.
words.exampleEN: My skin is dry in winter.
words.audioHint: The 'ui' sounds like 'ow' with rounded lips. The 'd' sounds like 't'.

words.exampleEN: I'm very tired today.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo' in 'moon'. Very short word.

words.exampleEN: I feel nauseous.
words.audioHint: The 'i' sounds like 'i' in 'miss'. The '-lijk' ending sounds like 'luk'.

words.exampleEN: I'm a bit dizzy.
words.audioHint: The 'ui' sounds like 'ow' with rounded lips. The 'g' at the end is guttural.

words.exampleEN: The wound needs to be cleaned.
words.audioHint: The 'w' sounds like 'v'. The 'd' at the end sounds like 't'.

words.exampleNL: Heb je een pleister voor me?
words.exampleEN: Do you have a plaster for me?
words.audioHint: The 'ei' sounds like 'eye'. Used for adhesive bandages.

words.exampleNL: De huisarts stuurt me door naar een specialist.
words.exampleEN: The GP refers me to a specialist.
words.audioHint: Similar to English. In the Dutch healthcare system, you need a GP referral to see a specialist.

words.exampleEN: The surgery went well.
words.audioHint: The 'tie' ending is pronounced 'tsee'. Stress on the third syllable.

words.exampleNL: Het eigen risico is dit jaar 385 euro.
words.exampleEN: The deductible is 385 euros this year.
words.audioHint: Important Dutch healthcare term. Everyone pays their 'eigen risico' before insurance covers costs.

words.exampleEN: The wound is still bleeding.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. Derived from 'bloed' (blood).

words.exampleEN: I bumped my elbow.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. The final 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleEN: I sprained my ankle.
words.audioHint: The 'e' sounds like 'e' in 'end'. The 'ng' like in 'ring'.

words.exampleEN: He has a broken rib.
words.audioHint: Same as English. Short 'i' sound.

words.exampleEN: I've had back pain for weeks.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'rug' (back) + 'pijn' (pain). The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'.
